planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and stream banks. Atlantic Goldenrod tolerates more shade than most goldenrods, making it useful for shaded or semi-shaded native plant gardens.

directions: Atlantic Goldenrod is a native perennial of moist, open woodlands, forest margins, and stream banks across the eastern United States, valuable as one of the few goldenrods that tolerates significant shade and moist conditions — making it a useful bridge between the sunny meadow and shaded woodland garden. The distinctive, open, arching inflorescence with well-separated clusters of bright yellow flowers differs from the more tightly packed plume of common goldenrod and is produced from August through October, providing late-season nectar for native bees, moths, and migrating monarch butterflies. A versatile native that thrives along shaded stream banks and moist woodland edges, Atlantic Goldenrod fills an ecological niche not covered by most other goldenrod species. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants spread modestly by rhizomes and are long-lived in appropriate moist, semi-shaded conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral, infertile soil in full sun. Hairy Goldenrod is well-adapted to dry rock outcrops, ledges, and sandy open areas and is particularly drought-tolerant among goldenrods once established.

directions: Hairy Goldenrod is a compact native perennial of dry, rocky or sandy open areas, shale barrens, rocky outcrops, and infertile upland soils across the northeastern United States, recognizable among goldenrods by its densely hairy (hispid) stems and leaves that give the entire plant a soft, grayish appearance. The small clusters of bright yellow flowers are produced on short, compact stems from August through September and attract native bees, small butterflies, and other pollinators visiting rocky, dry habitats where fewer plant species grow. Distinguished from other goldenrods by its smaller stature, hairy texture, and preference for lean, rocky, dry soils, Hairy Goldenrod is an excellent native choice for rock gardens, dry slopes, sandy paths, and xeriscapes where most goldenrods would languish. Direct sow seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; established plants are very drought-tolerant and require no supplemental irrigation or soil amendment.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, rocky, slightly acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in partial shade to full sun. Stout Goldenrod is native to rocky Appalachian woodland edges and tolerates partial shade better than most goldenrods.

directions: Stout Goldenrod is a native perennial of rocky Appalachian woodlands, cliff edges, shale barrens, and dry to moderately moist forest margins from New England south to the mountains of Tennessee and North Carolina, valued as one of the more shade-tolerant goldenrods and distinctive for its stout, robust stems and narrow, leafy inflorescence of bright yellow flowers in August and September. Unlike many goldenrods that require full sun and open meadows, Stout Goldenrod grows happily at shaded rock outcrops, rocky woodland edges, and forest margins with only partial sun, making it a useful native plant for challenging semi-shaded, rocky garden sites. The dense clusters of small yellow flowers attract native bees and late-season butterflies as one of the last significant nectar sources before fall in Appalachian woodland habitats. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, rocky, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are long-lived and require little maintenance.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, peaty, poorly drained soil in full sun to partial shade. Bog Goldenrod is specifically adapted to the saturated, acidic, nutrient-poor conditions of sphagnum bogs, fens, and wet meadows — it will not thrive in ordinary garden soil without significant moisture.

directions: Bog Goldenrod is a native perennial of the cool, acidic sphagnum bogs, fens, wet meadows, and peaty swamp edges of the northeastern United States and Canada, occupying a specialized ecological niche shared with carnivorous plants, bog orchids, and other acid-loving bog species that most other goldenrods cannot tolerate. The slender, wand-like spikes of small yellow flowers are produced in August and September in the open, sunny conditions of floating sphagnum mats and sedge fens, attracting late-season native bees and butterflies that visit these specialized wetland habitats. Bog Goldenrod provides an important native plant option for water gardeners, bog garden enthusiasts, and restorers working with cold northern wetland habitats. Direct sow seeds in fall in moist to wet, acidic, peaty, permanently saturated or nearly saturated soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants spread slowly by rhizomes in appropriate bog or fen conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun. Slender Blazingstar is adapted to the xeric, sandy coastal plain habitats of the Florida peninsula and adjacent states and will not thrive in clay, irrigated, or overly fertile garden conditions.

directions: Slender Blazingstar is a native perennial of the sandy coastal plain, pine flatwoods, and xeric scrub of the Florida peninsula and adjacent southeastern states, producing feathery, slender spikes of pink-purple flower heads on wiry stems in fall that are a critical nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies and native bees traversing the Florida peninsula during their southward fall migration. The narrow, thread-like leaves that cover the slender stems give the plant its common name; the flowers open from top to bottom on the spike, like all Liatris, and are notably fragrant compared to many other blazingstars. Adapted to the poor, sandy, xeric soils of the coastal plain where it often grows in open pine-palmetto flatwoods alongside other fire-adapted wildflowers, Slender Blazingstar requires excellent drainage and periodic burning or mechanical disturbance to maintain its competitive advantage. Direct sow seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; established corms are long-lived and drought-tolerant once settled in appropriate conditions.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich, poorly drained to seasonally wet soil in partial shade to full sun. Maleberry thrives in the wet, acidic conditions of pocosins, bog margins, and wet flatwoods where most other ornamental shrubs will not succeed.

directions: Maleberry is a native deciduous shrub of wet, acidic soils across the eastern United States, found in pocosins, bog margins, wet flatwoods, and seasonally flooded stream banks from Maine south to Florida and west to Texas. The clusters of small, urn-shaped white flowers in late spring attract native bees and bumblebees, while the attractive fall foliage ranges from yellow to orange-red in appropriate sunlight. A member of the blueberry family (Ericaceae), Maleberry shares the extreme soil requirements of its relatives — strongly acidic, humus-rich, poorly drained to saturated conditions that would stress most landscape shrubs. Well-suited for wet, acidic garden settings including bog gardens, rain garden overflow zones, and moist native plant hedges in acid soil conditions, Maleberry is rarely cultivated but deserves wider use for challenging wet, acidic sites. Plant rooted cuttings in strongly acidic, moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist, well-drained to moderately wet, strongly acidic, sandy to loamy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. All parts of Staggerbush contain grayanotoxins (andromedotoxins) and are poisonous to livestock — do not plant where grazing animals have access.

directions: Staggerbush is a graceful native deciduous shrub of the coastal plain's moist to moderately dry, acidic soils from New England south to Florida and west to Texas, producing elegant, pendulous clusters of white to pink, urn-shaped flowers in May that hang gracefully from the branches before the leaves fully expand. The common name 'staggerbush' refers to the plant's toxicity to sheep and other livestock that consumed the foliage; like all Lyonia species, the leaves contain grayanotoxins (andromedotoxins) that cause staggering, drooling, and potentially fatal poisoning in grazing animals. Despite this toxicity, Staggerbush is an attractive ornamental native shrub with graceful spring flowers, attractive summer foliage, and modest red to orange fall color that supports native bees when in bloom. Plant in moist, well-drained to moderately wet, strongly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; keep away from livestock.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade along stream banks or in seasonally wet, shaded woodland settings. Swamp Doghobble requires consistently moist, shaded conditions and performs best at the shaded edge of streams and wet woodland understories.

directions: Swamp Doghobble is a graceful native deciduous shrub of moist to wet, shaded, acidic habitats along shaded stream banks, boggy swamp margins, and wet woodland understories of the eastern United States, producing fragrant, white, cylindrical flowers in drooping clusters along the stems in spring before the leaves fully emerge. Unlike the evergreen leucothoes, Swamp Doghobble is deciduous and displays outstanding orange, red, and purple fall foliage color that rivals the showiest cultivated shrubs. The arching, fountain-like growth habit and multi-season ornamental interest — spring flowers, attractive summer foliage, and brilliant fall color — make Swamp Doghobble a highly desirable native shrub for shaded, moist, acid garden settings. Plant in strongly acidic, moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Swamp Doghobble will not tolerate alkaline soil, drought, or prolonged direct sun.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in permanently wet, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0), nutrient-poor, sphagnum peat bog or fen conditions in full sun to partial sun. Bog Rosemary requires exceptionally cool, wet, acidic growing conditions and is only suitable for northern bog gardens or cool, high-altitude peat-moss landscapes — it will not survive in warm, dry, or alkaline conditions.

directions: Bog Rosemary is a native circumpolar evergreen shrub of the coldest, wettest, most acidic sphagnum bogs and arctic tundra of the Northern Hemisphere, prized among bog garden enthusiasts for its attractive, narrow, blue-green, rosemary-like evergreen leaves with rolled margins and beautiful clusters of small, pink, globe-shaped flowers in spring. Like other members of the blueberry family adapted to the specialized bog environment, Bog Rosemary requires extreme growing conditions — permanently saturated, strongly acidic (pH 3.5–5.0), nutrient-poor peat — and is entirely unsuitable for conventional garden soil or climates south of Zone 6. All parts of the plant contain grayanotoxins and are toxic to livestock; the specific epithet polifolia ('gray-leaved') refers to the glaucous appearance of the foliage. Plant rooted cuttings in permanently wet sphagnum peat in full sun; Bog Rosemary is an outstanding native specimen for cold-climate bog gardens, peat gardens, and tundra-habitat restorations where its specialized conditions can be maintained.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in dry to moderately moist, strongly acidic, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. Coastal Plain Staggerbush is adapted to the xeric, fire-maintained scrub and flatwood habitats of the Florida peninsula and requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate clay, wet soils, or alkaline conditions.

directions: Coastal Plain Staggerbush is a native evergreen shrub endemic to the dry, sandy, fire-maintained scrub and flatwood habitats of the Florida peninsula, where its dense, leathery-leaved growth and small white to pink urn-shaped flowers in spring make it a characteristic component of the open, sunny, infertile sand communities dominated by oaks and palmettos. Adapted to the same xeric conditions as Florida Rosemary, Scrub Hickory, and other Florida scrub specialists, Coastal Plain Staggerbush requires excellent drainage and periodic low-intensity fire to maintain the open, competitive-free conditions it requires. Like other Lyonia species, the foliage contains grayanotoxins and is toxic to livestock if consumed. Plant in dry, strongly acidic, sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun — Coastal Plain Staggerbush thrives in the nutrient-poor, fire-managed conditions of its native habitat and requires no supplemental fertilizer or irrigation once established.
